beats.text: Start in discrete time, where a walker flips a fair coin at each tick and steps right on heads or left on tails.

visual.sub: The fair game at the spine of finance

beats.text: The best forecast of his next position is exactly where he stands now, the property that defines a martingale.

visual.note: A fair game: no past-only strategy tilts the expected outcome

beats.text: Increments are independent with mean zero and unit variance, so after n steps the position has variance equal to n.
Variance accumulates linearly in the steps, so the typical distance travelled grows like the square root of n.
After nine steps the variance is nine, so the standard deviation of the position equals three.
Refine the clock and take the limit, and the discrete stagger converges to continuous Brownian motion.
The limit of the rescaled walk is standard Brownian motion, the Wiener process, defined by four axioms.

visual.sub: The Wiener process and its four axioms

beats.text: It starts at zero, has Gaussian increments with variance equal to elapsed time, independent on disjoint intervals, and continuous paths.
Two consequences follow at once: the expected value is zero and the variance of W at time t is simply t.
The path is continuous but nowhere differentiable, since displacement over a tiny interval scales like the square root of dt.
Summed squared increments do not vanish like a smooth curve, but converge deterministically to t, so dW squared equals dt.

visual.note: The single most important line in the chapter

beats.text: At time four the variance of standard Brownian motion is four, the elapsed time measured in spread squared.

concepts.title: Geometric Brownian motion — the stock model

beats.text: A stock cannot follow plain Brownian motion, because prices would wander below zero and feel dollar rather than percentage shocks.
The fix puts noise on the returns: the instantaneous return earns a drift mu plus a noise scaled by sigma.

visual.note: Noise scales with S, so price cannot reach zero

beats.text: Because the log-price is Brownian with drift, the price itself is lognormal, never negative and right-skewed.
The log drift is mu minus half sigma squared, the Itô correction sometimes called volatility drag.

visual.note: Median compounded return falls below the mean by half the variance

beats.text: The mean still grows at the full rate mu, while the typical realized path sits near the lower median.

visual.note: The mean is dragged up by the fat right tail

beats.text: With a twenty percent volatility, the Itô correction is one-half times point-zero-four, equal to point-zero-two.
This is the heart of the chapter: the chain rule of stochastic calculus, proved by Kiyosi Itô in 1944.
Everything matches ordinary calculus except the half sigma squared second-derivative term, the trace left by quadratic variation.

visual.tex: \mathrm{d}f = \Big(\tfrac{\partial f}{\partial t} + \mu\tfrac{\partial f}{\partial x} + \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2\tfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2}\Big)\mathrm{d}t + \sigma\tfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}\mathrm{d}W_t
visual.note: Randomness adds a deterministic drift to any nonlinear function

beats.text: Applied to the log of a stock, the lemma yields the GBM drift of mu minus half sigma squared.

visual.note: The ½σ² is purely the Itô term

beats.text: For f equal to W squared, naive calculus says two W dW, but Itô adds a lone plus dt term.

visual.note: That +dt is the entire difference between the two calculi

beats.text: Taking expectations kills the martingale term, so the expected value of W squared equals t, not zero.
At time nine the expected value of W squared is nine, where naive calculus would wrongly say zero.
A stochastic differential equation writes the infinitesimal change of a process as a deterministic drift plus a random diffusion.

visual.sub: The master template of every model

beats.text: The drift function gives the local mean rate and the diffusion function the local volatility; choosing them is choosing the model.

visual.note: Constant a,b: Brownian; a=μx, b=σx: GBM

beats.text: Most SDEs have no closed form, so the Euler–Maruyama scheme steps forward with Gaussian noise scaled by the square root of dt.

visual.tex: X_{t+\Delta t} = X_t + a\,\Delta t + b\,\sqrt{\Delta t}\;Z
visual.note: Getting the √Δt exponent wrong is the most common Monte-Carlo bug

beats.text: The mean-reverting Ornstein–Uhlenbeck process is pulled toward a long-run level, and its variance saturates rather than growing forever.
Unlike Brownian motion whose spread grows without bound, the OU variance levels off at sigma squared over two kappa.

visual.note: Canonical model for anything that wanders but does not run away

beats.text: The idealization leaks where markets jump and tails fatten, so jump-diffusion and stochastic volatility extend Itô rather than abandon it.
